turns.speaker: host
turns.text: Developing a new drug typically takes over a decade and costs billions, with a tiny success rate. How is artificial intelligence stepping in to change this massive bottleneck?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: AI is being applied at every stage to cut costs and improve those low odds, starting with techniques like molecular docking and virtual screening. Deep learning scoring functions now help us predict how well a molecule binds to a target much better than older physics-based methods.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: I've heard we can now screen billions of molecules computationally, which sounds impossible without AI.

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: Libraries like Enamine REAL Space contain over 37 billion compounds, so we use graph neural networks to efficiently navigate this vast chemical space. Instead of explicitly docking every single molecule, these AI models learn to identify the most promising candidates based on structural patterns.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: Finding a molecule that binds is one thing, but don't most drugs fail later because of safety or how they behave in the body?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: Exactly, and that's where predicting ADMET properties—Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion, and Toxicity—becomes critical. Machine learning models can now rapidly evaluate these factors using molecular graphs, acting as filters to prevent costly late-stage failures.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: Beyond just filtering existing molecules, can AI actually invent completely new drugs?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: That's the most exciting frontier, known as generative chemistry, where models like diffusion networks and variational autoencoders design novel molecules from scratch. They can simultaneously optimize for binding affinity, solubility, and low toxicity, essentially automating de novo drug design.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: We can't talk about this without mentioning AlphaFold—how has predicting protein structures changed the game?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: AlphaFold has been profound because it provides accurate 3D protein structures from amino acid sequences alone, unlocking targets that lacked experimental data. While the predicted structures sometimes need refinement for flexible binding sites, they give us an incredible starting point for these AI-driven design campaigns.
